res-001.zusammenfassung: Adfree Cities UK measured the electricity consumption of various types of digital out-of-home advertising displays and compared them with household electricity usage.
res-001.kernbefund: A large digital advertising display (6.4 m × 3.1 m) consumes **41,627 kWh per year** — equivalent to the annual electricity consumption of 11 British households. A medium-sized display (1.2 m × 1.8 m, "Six Sheet") consumes 11,501 kWh/year (3 households). A double-sided unit in a city center consumes as much as 4 households. At night (10 pm–6 am), one large display consumes as much as 8.5 households during the same period.
res-001.relevanz: Every digital billboard in public space consumes energy around the clock — exclusively for commercial purposes. This energy consumption provides no social benefit and directly competes with the goals of the energy transition (Energiewende).

res-002.zusammenfassung: A city-wide measurement of the electricity consumption of digital advertising screens in Leeds (UK) by Adfree Cities. Compared with the electricity consumption of the city's population.
res-002.kernbefund: The digital advertising screens in Leeds consume a total of **4.1 GWh per year** — enough to power 3,300 residents. This is the measured electricity consumption solely for commercial out-of-home advertising in a medium-sized British city.
res-002.relevanz: This figure makes the cumulative costs of outdoor advertising tangible at a city scale: the advertising in a single city consumes as much electricity as a medium-sized town. Scaled up across all cities in Germany or Europe, the result is a massive and avoidable energy consumption.

res-003.zusammenfassung: During the energy crisis of 2022 (triggered by Russia's halt of gas supplies), Germany, together with Austria, Spain and France, mandated that digital advertising screens be switched off between 10 pm and 6 am. Berlin's Kurfürstendamm (a major commercial boulevard) and other high-visibility advertising sites went dark at night.
res-003.kernbefund: During the 2022 energy crisis, digital advertising displays in several EU countries were classified as non-essential energy consumers and shut down at night. This constitutes an explicit government acknowledgement that outdoor advertising ranks below essential energy consumers.
res-003.relevanz: When the state shuts down advertising screens first in a crisis, it implicitly confirms: outdoor advertising is not a public benefit, but a luxury consumption of resources that is immediately dispensable when supply is constrained.

res-004.zusammenfassung: Data from the EU-funded ECOBOARD project (CORDIS) and EnKad Sciences on the volume of waste generated by advertising banners and billboards.
res-004.kernbefund: In the United States alone, **600,000 tonnes of PVC advertising banner waste** are disposed of annually. In the Brussels region, 10,000 billboards generate **68,000 m² (approx. 680 tonnes) of vinyl waste** per year. Across Europe, more than **6 million m² of poster paper** is discarded — not recycled — **every 2 weeks**, containing environmentally harmful printing inks and toxic adhesives.
res-004.relevanz: Advertising banners consist of 33% PVC resin, 36% calcium carbonate, 18% polyester fiber, and 9% plasticizers (phthalates). This material requires over **1,000 years to decompose** in landfills. Outdoor advertising is one of the largest sources of short-lived PVC plastic waste in public space.

res-005.zusammenfassung: Greenpeace analysis of the toxicity of PVC (polyvinyl chloride) across its entire lifecycle. PVC is the material used in the vast majority of all outdoor advertising banners and poster backings.
res-005.kernbefund: PVC causes environmental and health damage at every stage: **Production** (PVC consumes 30–40% of all industrially produced chlorine; generates dioxins), **Use** (plasticizers/phthalates leach out and damage the hormonal system, reproduction, and respiratory tract), and **Disposal** (release of dioxins during incineration; 1,000+ years decomposition time in landfills). Phthalates from PVC are detected in waterways, air, and human tissue.
res-005.relevanz: Outdoor advertising banners are among the shortest-lived PVC products in existence: they are replaced every 4 weeks — for each new advertising campaign. The entire lifecycle of this toxic material serves exclusively the commercial purpose of capturing attention.

res-006.zusammenfassung: Scientific analysis of the chemical ingredients in printing inks used for large-format printing (as used for outdoor advertising and billboards). Printing inks are an often-overlooked component of the environmental costs of outdoor advertising.
res-006.kernbefund: Printing inks used for advertising contain as their primary solvents **toluene (approx. 70% of all emitted chemicals)**, as well as methyl ethyl ketone, xylene, and 1,1,1-trichloroethane — all volatile organic compounds (VOCs) and hazardous air pollutants (HAPs). Additionally: **cadmium, lead** (heavy metals), **bisphenol A (BPA)** (an endocrine disruptor), and synthetic antioxidants.
res-006.relevanz: Every advertising print releases these chemicals into the air — in printing facilities and through off-gassing from posters in public space. The production of advertising materials is an industrial chemical source that is restarted with every campaign change.

res-007.zusammenfassung: A peer-reviewed study assessing the external costs of outdoor advertising in Warsaw (Poland) by measuring residents' willingness to pay for a reduction in advertising.
res-007.kernbefund:
Warsaw residents were willing to pay:
- **€1.7–4.1 million/year** for a reduction in freestanding advertising structures
- **€5.5–9.3 million/year** for a reduction in building-mounted advertising
- An average of **€15–23 per person per year** for advertising reduction

These figures quantify the external costs of outdoor advertising that are currently borne entirely by the public and not compensated by the advertising industry.
res-007.relevanz: The study is the first to quantify the economic harm that outdoor advertising inflicts on urban residents — in the form of uncompensated external costs. Scaled up to major German cities, this represents a substantial societal cost factor.

res-008.zusammenfassung: An economic analysis of the attention economy as an external cost factor. Examines whether a Pigouvian tax on attention capture by advertising is justified.
res-008.kernbefund: According to APA (American Psychological Association) research, attention interruptions from advertising and other distractions destroy up to **40% of productive working time**. Multitasking and forced attention-switching deplete neural glucose reserves disproportionately. The authors propose a Pigouvian tax on attention capture — analogous to environmental taxes — because the cognitive costs are fully externalized: advertisers profit while those affected bear the costs.
res-008.relevanz: Outdoor advertising is designed to force involuntary attention (cf. psych-006). The associated cognitive costs are entirely externalized and borne by passersby without consent and without compensation.

res-010.zusammenfassung: Technical lifecycle analysis of digital LED billboards compared with traditional static billboards. Assesses manufacturing and operational emissions.
res-010.kernbefund: The **manufacture of a digital billboard** generates a **3.5 times larger CO₂ footprint** than the manufacture of a traditional static billboard. LED displays over 10 years can, however, save 18 tonnes of CO₂ in operation compared with LCD screens. The manufacturing phase alone means: every new digital billboard represents a substantial CO₂ investment that takes years to amortize.
res-010.relevanz: The global DOOH market is growing at 12% per year — new screens are being installed en masse. Every installation generates 3.5 times the manufacturing CO₂ footprint of a traditional billboard, before a single advertisement has been shown.

res-011.zusammenfassung: Despite the shift to LED technology, the absolute energy consumption of digital billboards (DOOH) remains immensely high, as they illuminate continuously around the clock, require cooling, and emit extremely high brightness.
res-011.kernbefund: Research confirms that a typical double-sided digital advertising screen at a bus stop consumes approximately 11,000 to 14,000 kWh of electricity per year. This is equivalent to the electricity consumption of three to four average households. This massive consumption of resources takes place in the midst of a global energy and climate crisis.
res-011.relevanz: DOOH is in direct contradiction to urban and national energy-saving targets. The "Net Zero" strategies promoted by the industry often only offset, but do not reduce, the exorbitant direct electricity consumption of the hardware.

safety-001.zusammenfassung: A comprehensive collection of studies by Scenic America shows that digital billboards (DOOH) divert drivers' gaze from the road significantly longer than static signs.
safety-001.kernbefund: A glance away from the road lasting more than two seconds — which is frequently triggered by changing or animated digital content — dramatically increases the risk of an accident. Studies conducted in the United States (including in Alabama and Florida) document elevated crash rates in the immediate vicinity of such installations.
safety-001.relevanz: DOOH installations are not merely visual pollution, but a concrete risk to public road safety. The profit interests of the advertising industry are in direct conflict with protecting road users.

safety-002.zusammenfassung: The advertising industry frequently cites studies (such as the 2013 FHWA study) to claim that digital billboards are traffic-safe. Independent reviews, however, reveal serious methodological flaws in these industry-supported investigations.
safety-002.kernbefund: Independent traffic safety experts (such as Jerry Wachtel) demonstrate that exculpatory studies often suffer from excessively small sample sizes, inadequate measuring instruments, and flawed experimental designs. Independent data consistently shows an elevated risk of cognitive and visual distraction.
safety-002.relevanz: The safety of DOOH advertising is being systematically downplayed through targeted lobbying and potentially flawed government studies.

safety-003.zusammenfassung: A real-world study commissioned by the Swedish Transport Administration examined 12 electronic billboards on a three-lane urban motorway section in Stockholm. The supported result is that drivers looked longer and more often at electronic billboards than at other signs on the same road sections.
safety-003.kernbefund: The authors report significantly longer dwell time, higher fixation count and longer maximum fixation duration for electronic billboards. Important caveat: the publication itself states that the data cannot conclusively answer whether the billboards constitute a traffic safety hazard; it supports a clear effect on gaze behaviour.
safety-003.relevanz: For DOOH, this is evidence of driver-attention capture, not proof of a blanket Swedish rural-road ban. Authorities should assess electronic billboards using gaze deviation, siting, speed environment, brightness, dwell time and crash context.

safety-004.zusammenfassung: The extreme luminance of digital billboards can impede the perception of critical traffic signals and emergency vehicles.
safety-004.kernbefund: It is well established in the psychology of visual perception that stronger light stimuli can mask weaker ones. High-power LED screens positioned in the line of sight to traffic signals or road signs can drastically reduce their signal effectiveness, particularly at night or in poor weather conditions (rain, fog). Furthermore, the enormous brightness and color dynamics of DOOH installations can cause the flashing warning lights of approaching emergency vehicles (blue light) to be lost in the visual noise of the surroundings, or to be perceived too late — dangerously extending the reaction time of road users.
safety-004.relevanz: Outdoor advertising poses a safety risk when it competes with official safety infrastructure for visual dominance. Prioritizing advertising messages can, in critical situations, endanger human lives.

safety-005.zusammenfassung: A peer-reviewed investigation of distraction effects on pedestrians in urban environments. Digital and animated outdoor advertising significantly increases visual distraction and raises the risk of accidents at intersections and crossings.
safety-005.kernbefund: Pedestrians and cyclists exposed to visually distracting stimuli (screens, moving image changes) show measurably delayed reaction times at road crossings, more frequent crossing without visual contact with traffic, and increased error rates in situation assessment. Digital advertising formats with high light intensity and moving images (DOOH) are more effective than static advertising in their distracting effect.
safety-005.relevanz: Unlike driver distraction from DOOH (which is widely documented), pedestrian and cyclist distraction has so far barely been addressed in DOOH regulation. In cities with high pedestrian and cycling density (Berlin, Hamburg, Amsterdam), risk exposure from DOOH screens at intersections is particularly relevant.

safety-006.zusammenfassung: Trafikverket had twelve digital billboards installed on a trial basis on a three-lane urban motorway section in Stockholm. An instrumented vehicle study with 41 drivers using eye-tracking documented longer and more frequent fixations on electronic billboards than on other road signs.
safety-006.kernbefund: The real-road, instrumented-vehicle, eye-tracking design is methodologically stronger than pure simulator work. The measurements showed longer dwell time, greater fixation count and longer maximum fixation duration for electronic billboards. At the same time, the publication found no effect on measured driving-performance variables and concluded cautiously that the safety-hazard question could not be answered conclusively from the available data.
safety-006.relevanz: The study is a strong warning signal that electronic billboards on high-speed roads capture driver attention. It should be used with crash studies and local site data, not as a stand-alone proof of increased crash rates or a documented removal order.

safety-007.zusammenfassung: A government-funded US study analysed 18 digital billboard locations in Alabama and Florida. The crash analysis found higher crash rates in digital-billboard influence zones compared with downstream control areas on the same routes.
safety-007.kernbefund: The study compared crash data at 18 DOOH sites with control points downstream. Result: the presence of digital billboards was correlated with increased crash rates in billboard influence zones, and sideswipe and rear-end crashes were overrepresented. Important caveat: the source frames the finding as correlation and notes that the relationship between digital billboards and traffic safety is complex.
safety-007.relevanz: Digital billboards on high-speed roads are a documented safety concern, but the precise supported wording is correlation with higher crash rates, not isolated causal proof. Road authorities should require site-specific crash analysis, sightline review, brightness limits and display-change controls before permitting DOOH near high-speed traffic.

urban-001.zusammenfassung: An empirical comparative study (UK and Brazil) on the negative effects of commercial signage and advertising on the visual quality of urban spaces and the quality of life of residents. Examined from architectural, planning, and psychological perspectives.
urban-001.kernbefund: Commercial signage and outdoor advertising have demonstrably negative consequences for the visual quality of urban spaces and the well-being of residents. The study establishes that visual overload from advertising acts as an environmental stressor.
urban-001.relevanz: The book is one of the most comprehensive scientific analyses of outdoor advertising as a source of visual pollution. It shows that advertising in public space destroys the character of cities and measurably reduces quality of life — irrespective of whether residents consciously perceive advertising or not.

urban-002.zusammenfassung: Development of a methodology for measuring visual pollution caused by outdoor advertising on urban streets, combining intervisibility analysis with public surveys. Demonstrates that over-stimulating advertising is a quantifiable source of visual pollution.
urban-002.kernbefund: Outdoor advertising is a measurable source of visual pollution on urban streets. Cities lack standardized measurement methods, which makes effective regulation impossible. The study provides, for the first time, a scientific tool for quantifying the problem.
urban-002.relevanz: The absence of measurement methods and standards allows the advertising industry to visually burden public space without limit, while cities are unable to effectively constrain this. The study shows that the problem is real, measurable, and amenable to regulation.

urban-003.zusammenfassung: A European comparative study on urban visual pollution, published in Nature Scientific Reports. Shows that visual pollution is far more than an aesthetic problem — it acts as an environmental stressor with direct health consequences.
urban-003.kernbefund: Visual pollution is a significant environmental problem driven by rapid urbanization. It is not merely an aesthetic nuisance, but acts directly on mental health: it contributes to anxiety, stress, and reduced quality of life. Advertising is one of the primary drivers of visual pollution in urban spaces.
urban-003.relevanz: In cities with high advertising density, residents demonstrably suffer from higher stress levels. Outdoor advertising is a primary, avoidable contributor to this burden.

urban-004.zusammenfassung: A critical urban analysis of outdoor advertising as a mechanism for privatizing public space. Examines how advertising imposes a specific "order" on the cityscape and undermines democratic control over public space.
urban-004.kernbefund: Advertising imposes a particular "spatial order" (Raumordnung) on public space that places commercial interests above public interests. Regulatory mechanisms operating under the label of "public accountability" serve commercial interests in practice. The "advertising city" dispossesses citizens of their shared urban space (urban commons).
urban-004.relevanz: Public space belongs to everyone — advertising turns it into an instrument of private profit interests. Citizens have no choice as to whether their fields of vision and attention are used for commercial purposes.

urban-005.zusammenfassung: A systematic literature review on visual pollution as an environmental problem. Identifies research gaps and methodological approaches to measurement and regulation. Published in 2024 in MDPI Land.
urban-005.kernbefund: Visual pollution is a recognized and growing environmental problem. Research shows consistent negative impacts on well-being and urban quality. Outdoor advertising is one of the primary drivers, yet it is barely captured by urban environmental regulations.
urban-005.relevanz: The study explicitly identifies outdoor advertising as a primary source of visual pollution and demonstrates that existing regulatory instruments are insufficient. It provides a comprehensive scientific framework for policy measures.

urban-006.zusammenfassung: The hardware design of so-called "double posters" (City-Light-Boards — freestanding double-sided advertising panels, typically found at transit stops) has far-reaching consequences for the architecture of public space, as they physically intrude into pavements and pedestrian zones.
urban-006.kernbefund: While traditional posters are usually mounted flat against walls, double-sided advertising installations (often 2 to 3 metres tall) are installed freestanding and perpendicular to the direction of pedestrian flow in order to guarantee maximum visibility from both directions. Studies show that this placement narrows pavements, restricts accessibility for wheelchair users, and cuts through urban sightlines.
urban-006.relevanz: The hardware of double-poster units privatizes actual physical space (the pavement) and subordinates pedestrians' freedom of movement to the visibility of advertising messages.

urban-007.zusammenfassung: Visual pollution from outdoor advertising does not merely harm aesthetics, but has directly measurable negative economic impacts on residents.
urban-007.kernbefund: Analyses of the US real estate market show that close proximity to commercial billboards (so-called "billboard blight") significantly reduces the value of adjacent residential properties. The contributing factors include light pollution from illumination or digital LEDs, the destruction of sightlines, and the psychological association of billboards with neglect and pure commercialization. This is particularly problematic because billboards have historically been concentrated disproportionately in already low-income neighborhoods (a pattern linked to "redlining" — the discriminatory practice of denying services to residents in minority neighborhoods).
urban-007.relevanz: Outdoor advertising redistributes wealth: advertising conglomerates generate profits while residents bear the real costs through noise, light, and declining property values (negative externalities).

urban-009.zusammenfassung: Digital Out-of-Home (DOOH) installations violate the boundaries of private space by emitting unsolicited light into homes and bedrooms.
urban-009.kernbefund: In urban planning, the phenomenon of "light trespass" (Lichteinbruch — the unwanted intrusion of light into private spaces) is regarded as a form of spatial boundary violation. DOOH screens often emit light in an undirected manner, illuminating the facades and interiors of nearby residents as if it were daylight. Because the light is commercial and often dynamic (changing images, flashing), it is psychologically harder to ignore than static street lighting. Affected residents report a massive reduction in quality of life and the feeling that, even within their own four walls, they can no longer escape the pressure of advertising.
urban-009.relevanz: Advertising conglomerates privatize citizens' attention by visually occupying their private spaces. The right to darkness and retreat in one's own home is subordinated to commercial interests.
